title: Incrementally Upgrading a RAG System
concept: A practical order of operations for improving a naive RAG system without a full rewrite.

exercises.prompt: In our last lesson, we categorized RAG optimizations. If you add a 'Reranker' to re-order documents after they are pulled from the database, which stage of the pipeline are you optimizing?
exercises.explain: The post-retrieval stage occurs after documents are fetched but before they are sent to the LLM. Reranking fits here perfectly because it re-orders the retrieved documents.

cards.title: Don't Rewrite, Refine
cards.body: When a RAG system starts failing, it's tempting to scrap everything and start over. But why throw away your entire setup when you can make surgical, high-impact upgrades to one piece at a time?

cards.title: Incremental Upgrade Strategy
cards.body: Incrementally upgrading RAG means making targeted, modular improvements to your pipeline. Instead of a complete rewrite, you isolate bottlenecks—like poor document parsing or weak search relevance—and fix them one step at a time.

cards.title: The Modular Tune-Up
cards.body: Think of upgrading RAG like modifying a car while keeping it running. Instead of building a brand-new engine, you swap the air filter (better parsing), then upgrade the fuel injector (smarter retrieval), and finally tune the exhaust (post-retrieval reranking).

cards.title: A Real-World Step-by-Step Fix
cards.body: A customer service bot is giving irrelevant answers. Instead of rewriting the whole app, the team adds a lightweight reranking step. This instantly prioritizes the most relevant text chunks before they reach the LLM, fixing the immediate quality issue.

cards.title: The Bigger Model Illusion
cards.body: Many developers think that fixing a poor RAG system requires choosing a newer, larger LLM. In reality, bad data in means bad data out; upgrading your data ingestion and search steps yields much better results than simply buying a bigger model.

cards.title: Rebuild vs. Plug-in
cards.body: Upgrading incrementally lets you swap out specific parts of your pipeline while keeping the core infrastructure stable.

left.text: Tear down the entire system and build a brand-new pipeline from scratch.

right.text: Keep the vector database, but plug in a reranker and rewrite user queries.

exercises.prompt: When prioritizing upgrades to a Naive RAG system, which of these is generally the best starting point to ensure high-quality search inputs?

options.text: Switching to a more expensive LLM API
Improving document parsing and chunking strategies
Deleting the vector store and using keyword search only

exercises.explain: Upgrading document parsing and chunking is a high-leverage first step because clean, well-structured text data directly improves the performance of all subsequent search and generation steps.

exercises.prompt: True or False: You must always re-index and re-embed your entire document collection whenever you want to improve a RAG system's accuracy.
exercises.explain: You can easily add pre-retrieval (like query rewriting) or post-retrieval (like reranking) steps without changing your underlying vector database or re-indexing your documents.

exercises.prompt: Fill in the missing word to describe how RAG modules should be designed for easy upgrades.
exercises.sentence: To upgrade a RAG system smoothly, you should keep the components isolated so you can test them individually.
exercises.explain: An incremental strategy relies on keeping steps isolated so you can test and upgrade one part of the system without breaking the rest.

exercises.prompt: Three developers suggest next steps for improving their RAG pipeline. Which one is an AI presenting a flawed, nonsensical recommendation?

options.text: 'Let's add a query expansion step to rewrite user questions with synonyms before searching.'
'We should add a cross-encoder reranker to evaluate the relevance of the retrieved chunks.'
'We should generate random queries to automatically delete low-scoring chunks from our database.'

exercises.explain: Option C demonstrates a classic AI hallucination/error, where it suggests a completely illogical step (generating random queries to delete data) instead of structured system improvements.

exercises.prompt: You want to upgrade your team's Naive RAG app. You want to avoid the high cost of re-embedding millions of documents. Which incremental step allows you to improve retrieval quality without rebuilding the vector index?

options.text: Re-embed the entire dataset with a brand-new model first, then change chunking sizes
Apply query expansion to incoming questions, then add a post-retrieval reranker over the retrieved chunks
Deploy a complex graph-based retrieval agent before optimizing the underlying text chunks
Replace the vector database with a relational database to bypass search issues entirely

exercises.explain: When improving an existing system, you start with the easiest, most foundational changes that require zero changes to your existing vector database. Upgrading chunking or query parsing (pre-retrieval) optimizes what goes in, while adding a reranker (post-retrieval) optimizes what comes out, all without the massive cost of re-embedding your entire database.
